verses.text: A new famine. Isaac heads to Gerar — almost exactly where his father had run when Egypt called. The LORD appears: do not go down to Egypt. Stay in this land.
A new famine. Isaac heads to Gerar — almost exactly where his father had run when Egypt called. The LORD appears: do not go down to Egypt. Stay in this land.

I will bless you. To you and your offspring I give all these lands — the covenant Abraham received now confirmed to you. Because Abraham obeyed me, kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes. The covenant passes on his father's obedience.

I will bless you. To you and your offspring I give all these lands — the covenant Abraham received now confirmed to you. Because Abraham obeyed me, kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes. The covenant passes on his father's obedience.

I will bless you. To you and your offspring I give all these lands — the covenant Abraham received now confirmed to you. Because Abraham obeyed me, kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes. The covenant passes on his father's obedience.

Isaac stays. But when the men of Gerar ask about Rebekah, he says — just like Abraham before him — she is my sister. The patriarch repeats his father's exact failure. Old fears pass down with old blessings.

Isaac stays. But when the men of Gerar ask about Rebekah, he says — just like Abraham before him — she is my sister. The patriarch repeats his father's exact failure. Old fears pass down with old blessings.
After a long time, Abimelech sees Isaac caressing Rebekah at a window. Surely she is your wife — why did you say sister? Isaac: lest I die for her. The lie wasn't very protective in the end.
After a long time, Abimelech sees Isaac caressing Rebekah at a window. Surely she is your wife — why did you say sister? Isaac: lest I die for her. The lie wasn't very protective in the end.

Abimelech is upset: someone in my house might have sinned with her unknowingly. He puts out a public decree — anyone who touches this man or his wife will die. The covenant is being protected even by pagan law.

Abimelech is upset: someone in my house might have sinned with her unknowingly. He puts out a public decree — anyone who touches this man or his wife will die. The covenant is being protected even by pagan law.

Isaac sows in that land and reaps a hundredfold the same year — extraordinary for that climate. The LORD blesses him. He grows great, then greater, then very great. Flocks, herds, servants — until the Philistines envy him.

Isaac sows in that land and reaps a hundredfold the same year — extraordinary for that climate. The LORD blesses him. He grows great, then greater, then very great. Flocks, herds, servants — until the Philistines envy him.

Isaac sows in that land and reaps a hundredfold the same year — extraordinary for that climate. The LORD blesses him. He grows great, then greater, then very great. Flocks, herds, servants — until the Philistines envy him.

The Philistines stop up every well Abraham had dug, filling them with dirt. And Abimelech tells Isaac directly: leave us, you have become mightier than we are. Sometimes God's blessing makes us unwelcome.

The Philistines stop up every well Abraham had dug, filling them with dirt. And Abimelech tells Isaac directly: leave us, you have become mightier than we are. Sometimes God's blessing makes us unwelcome.

Isaac moves to the valley of Gerar. He digs again the wells Abraham had dug, which the Philistines had filled in — and he calls them by the names his father gave them. He inherits both his father's struggles and his father's namings.

Isaac moves to the valley of Gerar. He digs again the wells Abraham had dug, which the Philistines had filled in — and he calls them by the names his father gave them. He inherits both his father's struggles and his father's namings.

His servants dig in the valley and find living water. The herdsmen of Gerar quarrel: the water is ours. Isaac names the well Esek — contention. They dig another; another quarrel. He names it Sitnah — hatred. Then a third, with no quarrel. He names it Rehoboth — room. For now the LORD has made room for us.

His servants dig in the valley and find living water. The herdsmen of Gerar quarrel: the water is ours. Isaac names the well Esek — contention. They dig another; another quarrel. He names it Sitnah — hatred. Then a third, with no quarrel. He names it Rehoboth — room. For now the LORD has made room for us.

His servants dig in the valley and find living water. The herdsmen of Gerar quarrel: the water is ours. Isaac names the well Esek — contention. They dig another; another quarrel. He names it Sitnah — hatred. Then a third, with no quarrel. He names it Rehoboth — room. For now the LORD has made room for us.

His servants dig in the valley and find living water. The herdsmen of Gerar quarrel: the water is ours. Isaac names the well Esek — contention. They dig another; another quarrel. He names it Sitnah — hatred. Then a third, with no quarrel. He names it Rehoboth — room. For now the LORD has made room for us.

Isaac goes up to Beersheba. That night the LORD appears: I am the God of your father Abraham; fear not, I am with you. He builds an altar, calls on the name of the LORD, pitches his tent — the family pattern repeats. And his servants dig yet another well.

Isaac goes up to Beersheba. That night the LORD appears: I am the God of your father Abraham; fear not, I am with you. He builds an altar, calls on the name of the LORD, pitches his tent — the family pattern repeats. And his servants dig yet another well.

Isaac goes up to Beersheba. That night the LORD appears: I am the God of your father Abraham; fear not, I am with you. He builds an altar, calls on the name of the LORD, pitches his tent — the family pattern repeats. And his servants dig yet another well.
Abimelech comes with his commander and friend. Isaac is wary: why have you come, given that you hated me and sent me away?
Abimelech comes with his commander and friend. Isaac is wary: why have you come, given that you hated me and sent me away?
We saw clearly that the LORD was with you, they say. Let there be a covenant between us. Isaac makes a feast, they eat, swear an oath, and part in peace. Old enemies become neighbors.
We saw clearly that the LORD was with you, they say. Let there be a covenant between us. Isaac makes a feast, they eat, swear an oath, and part in peace. Old enemies become neighbors.
We saw clearly that the LORD was with you, they say. Let there be a covenant between us. Isaac makes a feast, they eat, swear an oath, and part in peace. Old enemies become neighbors.
We saw clearly that the LORD was with you, they say. Let there be a covenant between us. Isaac makes a feast, they eat, swear an oath, and part in peace. Old enemies become neighbors.

That same day, his servants find water in the new well. He names it Shebah — meaning oath, and also seven. The city is therefore called Beersheba unto this day. God's blessing arrives with the peace He had just sealed.

That same day, his servants find water in the new well. He names it Shebah — meaning oath, and also seven. The city is therefore called Beersheba unto this day. God's blessing arrives with the peace He had just sealed.

At forty, Esau marries two Hittite women. They become a grief of mind — bitterness of spirit — to Isaac and Rebekah. The son who despised his birthright is now choosing wives from outside the covenant. The wound deepens.

At forty, Esau marries two Hittite women. They become a grief of mind — bitterness of spirit — to Isaac and Rebekah. The son who despised his birthright is now choosing wives from outside the covenant. The wound deepens.
